next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

By eleven the sixth-floor window had gone the color of dishwater. Lakebridge had entered the wet part of cold, not snow yet, just a steady damp that climbed into coat sleeves and stayed there. Leela was trying to coax one dead benchmark run into becoming a paragraph she could still someday call a dissertation when the email from Research Integrity arrived with a cheerful blue flag beside it, as if someone had taken the time to mark it important by hand.

Subject: Required Attendance - Research Integrity Intake

Dear Ms. Krishnan,

Please report to Baines Hall, Room 414, at 2:30 p.m. today regarding a data-handling concern associated with privileged access credentials under your stewardship.

The message was signed by Mara Sloane, Associate Director, and copied an Information Security address that looked automated enough to absolve everybody in advance.

Leela read it twice, then a third time with her hand flat on the desk so the tendons in her wrist would stop twitching under the brace. The phrase privileged access credentials under your stewardship sat in her head like a stain. Nobody in Keating said things like stewardship unless they were already arranging blame into folders.

Across the cubicle wall Omar said, "You made the face."

He had a pharmacy bag open on his desk and was eating pistachios one by one, setting the empty shells in a neat line along the edge of a conference flyer. Even hungry, even frightened, he organized his mess before it existed. He leaned back enough to see her over the partition, his beard line still too careful for a man who had s...
open_hooks_to_carry: Shift the novel's rhythm from fluorescent public shame to greasy late-night aftermath.
Use humor and appetite as forms of temporary reprieve, not as tone-breaking gags.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e room.

June picked up the laptop. For a second she held it low against her stomach the way somebody might hold an ice pack to a bruise they didn't want to show. Then she straightened.

Leela said her name again, softly this time, because she had not earned anything louder.

June did not look at her. "Watch and learn," she said.

Then she went back in.

The room had resettled itself while she was out. People were back in their chairs with the obscene efficiency of academics who believed sitting down again restored innocence. Someone had opened the bakery box all the way. Half the cardamom knots were gone. Professor Adler had her pen ready. Ben had angled his laptop farther from the aisle.

June plugged in, pulled up the appendix, and stood at the podium under the fluorescent panels that flattened everyone's skin to the color of old paper. Leela stayed by the door for a beat before forcing herself back to her seat. The chair scraped louder than it should have. Nobody turned.

June put both hands on either side of the podium, not gripping, just placing them there like measurements.

"Okay," she said.

The word came out lower than before, stripped clean.

"Appendix first, since we're apparently doing surgery before triage. These are the thread-grouping notes from August. This is the author-linking mess. This is where the cleaner split changes the score, and this is where it doesn't. After that I'll finish the architecture and the deployment cases, because those still exist even if the room got bored of them."

She clicked to the next slide.

"So," she said, and kept speaking.
state_deltas_required: Leela and Omar try to interpret the seminar disaster and decide whether Mohit's behavior is strategic cruelty or simply ordinary academic bad faith.
They form a partial alliance built on incomplete truth, which helps them act together later but leaves a fracture inside the alliance from the start.
